title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the specific context of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant waterway flowing through our rohe. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu has maintained kaitiakitanga practices here for generations, examining historical land use changes, current restoration efforts led by local hapū, and the ongoing relationship between the community and this taonga.
learningObjectives: Analyze how historical land use decisions by both Māori and Pākehā have impacted the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Evaluate the effectiveness of current kaitiakitanga practices being implemented by local hapū to restore the mauri of the awa.
Collaboratively design and present a culturally-informed action plan that addresses a specific environmental challenge facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Articulate the personal and collective responsibilities of rangatahi in upholding the kaitiakitanga legacy for future generations.

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa (Connecting to the Stream)
activities.description: Begin with a karakia and mihi to the awa. Students will visit a designated safe section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Using sensory observation (seeing, hearing, touching), they will record their initial perceptions and sketch a section of the riverbank, noting both natural and human-made features.

activities.title: He Kōrero Tuku Iho: Pūrākau o te Awa (Historical Narrative)
activities.description: In the classroom, students will engage with resources including a timeline of significant events for the awa (e.g., pre-colonial health, arrival of settlers, agricultural intensification, recent restoration projects). They will work in small groups to create a 'cause and consequence' map for a specific period, identifying the social, economic, and cultural drivers behind environmental changes.

activities.title: Kanohi ki te Kanohi: Hui with a Kaitiaki (Face to Face Meeting)
activities.description: A local kaitiaki from a Mangakōtukutuku hapū will be invited to speak to the class. Students will prepare questions in advance about their role, the challenges they face, the successes of current projects, and their vision for the future of the awa. This emphasizes the living nature of kaitiakitanga.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi: Designing an Action Plan (Collaborative Work)
activities.description: In groups, students will choose a specific issue affecting the awa (e.g., sediment runoff, invasive weed species, rubbish). They will research this issue and develop a detailed, culturally-responsive action plan. The plan must include: a clear goal, proposed actions rooted in both mātauranga Māori and modern science, identified community partners, and a rationale for why their approach constitutes effective kaitiakitanga.

resources: Local Kaitiaki: A representative from the Mangakōtukutuku Marae Committee or a nearby hapū environmental group.
Digital Resource: The 'Land Air Water Aotearoa' (LAWA) website's specific page on the Mangakōtukutuku Stream water quality data.
Primary Source: Historical photographs of the Mangakōtukutuku area from the local museum or library archives.
Text: Excerpts from 'Te Mana o te Wai: A practical guide' from the Ministry for the Environment, focusing on case studies.

tasks: Submit the completed 'cause and consequence' map from Activity 2, demonstrating understanding of historical impacts.
Deliver a 5-minute group presentation pitching their Action Plan (from Activity 4) to the class and invited kaitiaki, justifying their proposed solutions.
Write an individual reflection (300-400 words) on what kaitiakitanga means to them personally after this unit and one action they will commit to.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L5: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.
Social Studies L5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people’s lives.
Social Studies L5: Understand how cultural interaction impacts on cultures and societies.
Social Studies L5: Understand how people define and seek human rights.
